title: Vibe Coding as a Product Manager
author: Netra Chandramohan
text:
Vibe Coding as a Product Manager
Over the past few months, I’ve noticed a meaningful shift in how quickly I can move from an idea to a working artifact.
Not because product work has become easier, but because the iteration cycle has tightened. When the cost of trying something drops, you spend less time debating hypotheticals and more time validating behavior. For a product manager, that changes the quality of decision-making: assumptions surface earlier, edge cases show up sooner, and trade-offs become concrete instead of theoretical.
I’ve been experimenting with what people broadly call “vibe coding” by building in small loops, staying close to the problem, and iterating directly against the output. I don’t view this as a replacement for the fundamentals of product work (user understanding, strategy, prioritization, and alignment). I view it as a way to accelerate the part of the work that benefits most from speed: early validation.
In practice, the biggest impact isn’t writing more code. It’s compressing the distance between intent and execution. When you can test an idea quickly, you naturally raise the bar for clarity. Once you run a prototype, unclear requirements surface immediately. You become more disciplined about defining what the product should do, what it should never do, and what “good” looks like. That discipline is familiar to PMs. It’s the same muscle used to define requirements, constraints, and acceptance criteria, just exercised in a tighter loop.
That loop is what led me to build a small product.
The problem that was worth prototyping
Most professionals already maintain their story across multiple sources: a resume, a LinkedIn profile, writing on Medium, projects on GitHub, Kaggle, and more. The challenge isn’t a lack of information. It’s fragmentation and drift. Each artifact is updated at different times, in different tones, and for different audiences. Anyone trying to understand the full picture has to stitch it together manually.
Website builders solve layout and hosting. They don’t solve the recurring, high-friction work of consolidating information, producing coherent summaries, and keeping a narrative consistent over time. That’s a classic product problem: high value, recurring, and too time-consuming for the value it delivers.
So I built a product that treats existing professional artifacts as the source of truth and generates a portfolio website from them.
What I built (first version)
I built an initial version that turns an existing professional footprint into a portfolio site without starting from a blank template.
Instead of asking users to retype the same information in yet another place, the product takes inputs most people already maintain: a resume plus LinkedIn, Medium, and GitHub URLs. It does the consolidation work by extracting key facts, reducing redundancy, and generating a coherent narrative across sections.
The objective is straightforward: reduce time to a shareable portfolio and remove the blank-page burden, while keeping the output easy to edit and refine.
This is still early. There are areas I want to make more evidence-driven and configurable, especially around how skills are supported and how the narrative adapts to different audiences. But it already proves the core workflow: a portfolio can be generated from existing sources in minutes, not days.
Codex was the tool I used most in the build loop. It performed well on implementation-heavy work: wiring the flow end to end, handling parsing logic, and iterating quickly as real inputs exposed edge cases. That reliability mattered because the product lives or dies on how it behaves with messy data, not ideal samples.
Where Codex was less efficient for me was front-end polish. It can generate UI, but it often takes more iteration to land on a layout that feels intentional, including hierarchy, spacing, and the details that make a site feel designed rather than assembled.
In those moments, I found Claude to be a stronger collaborator. It tended to converge faster on structure, presentation, and user-facing narrative. For front-end direction, content hierarchy, and overall readability, it produced better first drafts in my experience.
What I will improve next
As I continue building beyond the first version, my focus is on making the output more trustworthy, easier to keep up to date, and better aligned with how portfolios are actually reviewed.
First, I want to strengthen credibility. The site shouldn’t just list skills or produce summaries. It should be clear what those claims are based on, and the writing should feel specific and grounded rather than generic.
Second, I want to make staying current effortless. A portfolio loses value when it goes stale, so updates should be lightweight and driven by changes in the underlying sources rather than repeated manual rewrites.
Finally, I’m exploring role-aware curation. A portfolio that works for a growth-focused role doesn’t always highlight the same signals as a platform or AI-focused role. I want the system to emphasize the most relevant parts of a user’s footprint based on what they’re targeting, without changing the underlying facts.
This work is still early and the details will evolve, but the direction is stable: make it easier to turn scattered artifacts into a coherent portfolio that stays accurate over time.
